coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots assisted synthesis of highconcentration nitrogen doped graphene for infrared photodetectors
coredata.dc:creator: Xie, Feng
Liu, Zhiduo
Wang, Changxing
Chen, Da
Zhu, Wei
Li, Xiamen
Guo, Qinglei
Wang, Gang
coredata.dc:description: The application of graphene in the field of microelectronics is becoming more and more urgent with the emergence of bottlenecks in silicon-based semiconductor technology, and the ability of controllable doping in graphene is, therefore, strongly demanded to tune their electronic or optoelectronic properties for the fabrication of high-performance devices. Herein, through seeding zero-dimensional (0D) nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) on a catalytic substrate, the graphene monolayer with nitrogen doping is obtained via chemical vapor deposition (CVD). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) characterization shows the doping concentration reaches up to 32%. Experimental and theoretical investigations reveal that N-GQDs act as the nucleation sites for the epitaxial growth of doped graphene monolayers. Finally, infrared photodetector built on N doped graphene (NG) film is fabricated, accompanying with high detectivity (~1.52 × 1010 cm Hz1/2 W −1) and responsivity (79 mA W −1) at the wavelength of 1550 nm. Our study may provide a controllable and convenient approach to achieve doped graphene, which paves the way for the application of graphene in the field of microelectronics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
N-doped graphene
Chemical vapor deposition
Photodetector

C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University and the Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo under Grant (No. 2017A610104 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of the process flow for synthesizing NG film. (b) TEM image of the N-GQDs seeds, and the inset is the size distribution. (c) HR-TEM image of the N-GQDs seeds. (d) FFT image for N-GQDs. (e) The contact mode AFM image of the N-GQDs seeds, and the inset is the height of N-GQDs. (f) Raman spectra of PG film, N-GQDs, and NG film, respectively. (g) The AFM image of NG film, and the inset represents the corresponding thickness. (h) Raman mapping of I2D/IG of the NG film. (i) STM topographical image of NG film on a Cu foil substrate (V bias = 300 mV, Iset = 200 pA). (j) Line-cutting profile across a N dopant in (i).

(a) Raman spectra of NG films synthesized with different spin-coating speeds (900–750 rpm) of N-GQDs. The Raman shift of G bands (b) and 2D bands (c) of NG film varying with the spin-coating speed. Column diagram of the ID/IG (d) and the 2D bands FWHM (e) of NG film synthesized with different spin-coating speeds. (f) XPS spectra of the PG film and NG film. (g) Deconvolutions of the C 1s peak of PG film. Deconvolutions of the C 1s peak (h) and the N 1s peak (i) of NG film.
AFM images of NG films at different growth times: (a) 0 min, (b) 0.5 min, (c) 1 min, (d) 2 min, (e) 3 min, (f) 4 min, (g) 5 min. (h) Raman spectra of NG films at different growth times.

Electrical properties of NG film. (a) Colored SEM image of the GFETs. (b) IDS-VG curve of NG film based GFET annealed for 0, 12, 24, 36 h, and PG-based GFET are measured for comparison. (c) The neutral point of NG film based GFET varying with the annealing time. (d) The histogram of neutral point measured from 25 GFETs.

Optoelectronic properties of the photodetector fabricated with NG film. (a) Schematic diagram of the NG-based photodetector. (b) IDS-VDS of the photodetector measured with and without the light illumination (1550 nm). (c) Photoswitching response of the photodetector to a pulsed laser (1550 nm) under a bias voltage of 0.1 V. (d) Photoswitching response of the photodetector to a 1 kHz light illumination. (e, f) Magnified photoresponse curves for extracting rise and fall times.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots assisted synthesis of highconcentration nitrogen doped graphene for infrared photodetectors

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of ASIC and Systems

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: Q. Guo, School of Microelectronics, Shandong University, Jinan 250100, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The application of graphene in the field of microelectronics is becoming more and more urgent with the emergence of bottlenecks in silicon-based semiconductor technology, and the ability of controllable doping in graphene is, therefore, strongly demanded to tune their electronic or optoelectronic properties for the fabrication of high-performance devices. Herein, through seeding zero-dimensional (0D) nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) on a catalytic substrate, the graphene monolayer with nitrogen doping is obtained via chemical vapor deposition (CVD). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) characterization shows the doping concentration reaches up to 32%. Experimental and theoretical investigations reveal that N-GQDs act as the nucleation sites for the epitaxial growth of doped graphene monolayers. Finally, infrared photodetector built on N doped graphene (NG) film is fabricated, accompanying with high detectivity (~1.52 × 1010 cm Hz1/2 W −1) and responsivity (79 mA W −1) at the wavelength of 1550 nm. Our study may provide a controllable and convenient approach to achieve doped graphene, which paves the way for the application of graphene in the field of microelectronics.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene, known as the two dimensional honeycomb lattice of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms, is considered to be the promising candidate for the construction of various electronic devices, such as transistors, optoelectronics, sensors, and etc. [1–4] Since its first introduction by Geim and Novoselov in 2004 [1,5], graphene has been widely studied either from the fundamental aspect or from the engineering aspect. However, the electrical, optical and chemical properties of pristine graphene are limited by the absence of a band gap [6,7]. Therefore, various methods are proposed to open-up or optimize the band gap of graphene for further applications, such as forming heterostructures with substrates, doping graphene with other elements or adding pre-seed to assist graphene growth [8–10]. Among these initiatives, adding pre-seed, like oxidized graphene [9] and coronene [10], has become an extremely effective way to open the band gap of graphene. The seed layer can serve as the nucleation sites, which can shorten growth time and improve film uniformity. Li et al. demonstrated that graphene oxide seeds sowed on a copper foil are able to prepare uniformly doped graphene [9]. Other materials, for example coronene [10], are also utilized as seeds to prepared uniformly doped graphene. In terms of fabricating graphene-based optoelectronic devices, however, large-sized seeds are generally best avoided due to the accompanying larger defects and reduced carrier mobility of the doped film [10]. Therefore, it is highly desired to find a new seed with small size to improve the optoelectronic and electronic properties of graphene.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are widely investigated especially for applications in optoelectronic devices due to their distinctive merits, such as excellent photostability, low toxicity, eco-friendly preparation and widely available precursors [11–14]. Previously, our group have demonstrated that GQDs can be used as seeds during the growth process of doped graphene. For example, Li et al. used N & S dual-doped GQDs as pre-seeds to synthesize n-type graphene with a low doping concentration at high temperature on a copper substrate [15]. Hu et al. successfully synthesized n-type graphene with a high doping concentration, assisted by N & P dual-doped GQDs [16]. However, the CS bond related to the feature chemical bond of n-type graphene has a rather weak bond energy, which is prone to break at high temperature [17,18]. Therefore, the chemical vapor deposition (CVD) process at high temperature always results in a low doping level of graphene. In addition, P and S atoms are much larger than C atoms, and inevitably, these dopants will introduce more defects in the graphene film [19,20]. Among numerous potential dopants, N is considered to be an excellent candidate because of its similar atomic size to carbon and strong covalent bond with carbon. Therefore, stable and high doping concentration of N, with reduced defects compared to other dopants, is available in graphene. According to theoretical studies, N doping in graphene enhances the electron density, and adjusts the Fermi level (Ef) and chemical reaction properties. Moreover, in the various CN structures, n-type graphene realized by the direct substitution with graphitic N preserves a much higher charge carrier mobility due to the elimination of defect [21], which is important for high speed electronics. Thus, graphitic N is attractive to synthesize n-type graphene among other impurities.

Herein, we describe a well-controlled and tunable approach for the synthesis of N-doped graphene (NG) film with high concentration by using 0D N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) as the pre-seeds on a catalytic substrate through dynamic CVD. Through the initial seeding of N-GQDs, the concentration of dopant in graphene can be controlled and identified. The observation reveals that by seeding N-GQDs, NG film with high dopant concentration (32%) is achieved. The obtained doped graphene exhibits typical n-type semiconductor characteristics. Finally, a photodetector was prepared by the transfer of n-type graphene to SiO2/Si substrate, which shows excellent performance at 1550 nm wavelengths with high detection (~1.52 × 1010 cm Hz1/2 W−1) and responsivity (79 mA W−1). A relatively gentle route is proposed by combining locally controllable nucleation particles and in-situ CVD to synthesize tunable doped graphene film, which lays the foundation for the application of graphene in high-performance electronic and optoelectronic devices.

Fig. 1a illustrates the synthesis process for the NG film. A 99.8% pure copper (Cu) foil purchased from Alfa Aesar (with the thickness of 125 μm) is polished through mechanical and electrochemical method for decreasing the surface roughness. Cu foil is annealed at 1000 °C at low pressure with an H2 flow of 10 sccm for 30 min. Then, the N-GQDs are spin-coated on the annealed Cu foil to serve as harmless dopant sources, followed by placing it at the center of the horizontal quartz tube. Prior to heating, the quartz tube is evacuated to approximately 10−5 mbar and then filled with 200 sccm argon (Ar, 99.9999% purity) and 10 sccm H2 until reaching atmospheric pressure. After heating to the proper temperature (1000 °C) and filling with 5 sccm methane, the Cu substrates with the N-GQDs seeds are annealed for 5 min. Finally, the furnace is cooled to room temperature under flowing Ar (200 sccm) and H2 (10 sccm) atmosphere. Detailed description of the synthesis is given in Figs. S1–S4, in the Supporting Information.

The graphene/Cu substrate is spin-coated with PMMA (MicroChem 950 PMMA C, 2% in chlorobenzene), and heated at 180 °C for 20 min. Afterwards, the Cu foil is etched in an aqueous solution of ammonium persulfate ((NH4)2S2O8) allowing the PMMA/NG film to float on top of the solution. The PMMA/NG film could then be transferred to a foreign substrate for analysis and characterization. After washing with deionized water, the PMMA/NG film is transferred to the SiO2/Si substrate and annealed at 180 °C for 30 min to improve the adhesion with the substrate. PMMA is then dissolved gradually with acetone, and the carbonaceous layers are washed with isopropanol and dried with nitrogen (N2) gas. Finally, the NG film is annealed in Ar (10 sccm) and H2 (50 sccm) for 2 h at 300 °C to remove PMMA residues. Raman spectra of NG film before and after the transfer are shown in Fig. S5 in the Supporting Information, indicating that no defect is introduced during the transfer process.

Raman scattering is performed on a micro-Raman system (SENTERRA) with a 532 nm excitation laser. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) (Oxford Instruments, Cypher S) is used to determine the uniformity and thickness of the NG film. XPS (PHI 5802, Physical Electronics Inc., Eden Prairie, MN) is employed to determine the chemical composition. Crystallographic information is acquired by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (FET-Tecnai G2F20 S-7WIN). Scanning tunneling microscopy (STM) measurement is performed under a constant current after applying the voltage bias to samples. The transmission spectra of the sample are obtained by UV-vis-NIR spectrometry (Cary 5000). The photoelectric analyses are achieved under ambient conditions with a semiconductor parameter analyzer (B1500A, Agilent).

TEM is performed to characterize the morphology and size of N-GQDs. As shown in Fig. 1b, N-GQDs as the seeding material have rounded shapes, with a uniform size distribution of 2–5 nm, and the average size is estimated as 3 nm. Fig. 1c displays a typical high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image of as-prepared N-GQDs, which shows the high crystallinity with the lattice parameter of 0.24 nm. The fast Fourier transform (FFT) of the N-GQDs in Fig. 1d shows a six fold symmetry crystalline structure. Fig. 1e displays a representative AFM image of N-GQDs, which are highly dispersed and spin-coated on SiO2/Si substrate. The thickness of N-GQDs is approximately 1.2 nm, and the inset provides evidence that the layer number of N-GQDs is about 1–3. The chemical properties characterization details of N-GQDs are described in Fig. S6 in the Supporting Information. Raman spectra of the N-GQDs are illustrated in Fig. 1f, and NG film and pure graphene (PG) film are also characterized for comparison. There are three primary peaks, i.e., D-band at ~1350 cm−1, G-band at ~1580 cm−1 and 2D-band at ~2700 cm−1 from the Raman result of N-GQDs. Compared with PG film, an enhanced D-band appeared in NG, which is due to the fact that the in-plane doping of N atoms in hexagonal lattices of graphene could induce a faint defects [22]. Furthermore, a peak around 1620 cm−1 (denoted as D′) appears at the shoulder of G band. This D′ band is ascribed to an intravalley double-resonance process caused by defects, which further indicates the formation of structural defects. The intensity ratios of 2D and G peaks (I2D/IG) are larger than 1.5, which indicates the formation of monolayer graphene. The layer number can be further conformed by AFM measurement. As shown in Fig. 1g, the thickness of NG film is about 0.65 nm, indicating that the synthesized NG is monolayer. To determine the uniformity of layer number of the synthesized NG, the Raman mapping of the band intensity ratios of I2D/IG over a 5 μm × 5 μm area with a step size of 1 μm has been performed. As shown in Fig. 1h, I2D/IG mainly locates in the range of 1.9–2.4 (see Supporting Information Fig. S7), suggesting the formation of uniform monolayer graphene film. STM measurements are performed on the NG film to obtain the direct visualization of N dopants and their atomic configurations. As shown in Fig. 1i, an atomically resolved topography of the NG film is obtained, indicating that the addition of N-GQDs seeds enable the formation of graphene undergo sufficient morphology or microstructure transformation from 0D to 2D. More importantly, some bright “protrusions” within honeycomb lattices can be seen from the STM image, suggesting that the dopants (N) in NG film exist in a substituted manner. The line profile across the dopant yields the atomic corrugation with the maximum out-of-plane, as shown in Fig. 1j.

The dopant concentration of the synthesized NG film can be regulated by tuning N-GQDs spin-coating speed. Fig. 2a shows representative Raman spectra of NG films with different spin-coating speeds of N-GQDs. As the spin-coating speed decreases from 900 to 750 rpm, the 2D band of NG film shifts from ~2695 to ~2685 cm−1 and the G band shifts from ~1585 to ~1589 cm−1. It exhibits a G peak with higher wavenumber and a 2D peak with lower wavenumber compared to those of undoped graphene (Fig. 2b, c and Fig. S8) [23]. In addition, ID/IG and the full width half maximum (FWHM) of 2D band will increase simultaneously with the increase of N doping concentration in NG film (Fig. 2d and e). As shown in Fig. 2f, the peaks at 284.8 and 401 eV can be assigned to C 1s and N 1s, respectively. In the PG film, no meaningful N related signal is detected, whereas in the NG film, N 1s is apparently detectable. The N atomic concentration was calculated by taking the ratio of the total area under the N 1s spectrum to that under the C 1s one, and the doping level of N is estimated as 32%. In the high resolution scan (Fig. 2g), the C 1s peak can be divided into two sub peaks, i.e., sp2-C at ~284 eV and sp3-C at ~285.4 eV. As shown in Fig. 2h, three sub peaks are found in the C 1s peak of NG film, i.e., the sp2-C at ~284 eV, the sp3-C at ~285.4 eV and the CN at ~287.1 eV. The deconvolution of high-resolution N 1s peak, as shown in Fig. 2i, also appears three sub peaks, i.e., pyridinic N at ~398.8 eV, pyrrolic N at ~400.2 eV and graphitic N at ~400.9 eV. Fig. S9 shows XPS spectra of NG films synthesized with different spin-coating speed of N-GQDs. As the speed decreases from 900 to 750 rpm, the atomic percentage of N increases from 9.2% to 17%, 25.1% and 32%, respectively. Detailed N binding configuration was listed in Table S1, and the content of graphitic N is the highest, which is attributed to the occupation of N in the hexatomic ring of N-GQDs. The N 1s peak in the XPS spectrum of GQDs also confirms above results, which indicates that most of the N atoms are in situ doping. To determine the effect of spin-coating speed of N-GQDs on the thickness of the synthesized NG film, the corresponding transmittance of NG film on quartz substrate at 550 nm is measured, as shown in Fig. S10. As the spin-coating speed increases from 750 to 900 rpm, the optical transmittance of NG film gradually increases, but no less than 96%. Therefore, it can be inferred that the NG film only has one layer regardless of the doping concentration, which agrees well with the Raman results. The large value of optical transmittance also suggests the immense potential of our synthesized NG film in transparent electronics [24].

The nucleation and growth mechanism of NG films grown via dynamic CVD are investigated. Firstly, N-GQDs pre-seed is spin-coated on a polished Cu foil, and the tiny nuclei can be observed randomly on the foil surface (Fig. 3a). Then the obtained Cu foil is annealed in a mixture of H2 and Ar, and the edges of N-GQDs would generate rich dangling bonds, providing a solid foundation for the subsequent nucleation of the NG film. During the epitaxial process, the adsorbed carbon preferentially migrates and condenses to form nano-islands with dangling bonds, which facilitates the growth of NG film once the CH4 precursor is introduced [25]. In the first 0.5 min of the growth process (Fig. 3b), the spatial arrangement of NG domains on Cu substrate is uniform with an average size of about 160 nm (Fig. S11a). It is worth noting that some bright N-GQDs can be found on hexagonal NG domains, which confirms that N-GQDs are inserted into graphene. When the deposition time is increased to 1 min, the sizes of the NG domains significantly increase, and the average size is about 350 nm, as shown in Fig. 3c and Fig. S12b. After 2 min growth, hexagonal NG domains grow to about 1 μm, as shown in Fig. 3d and Fig. S11c. The formation of hexagonal NG facets is determined by the minimization of perimeter as well as energy [26–28]. From the enlarged AFM image (Fig. S12), the morphology of a single hexagonal NG sheet can be observed. Subsequently, the single-crystal NG domains start to contact with each other (3 min, Fig. 3e), and clear grain boundaries are observed as shown in Fig. 3f (4 min). Finally, a continuous NG monolayer is formed when the growth time extends to 5 min, as shown in Fig. 3g. Raman spectra of the samples at different growth time are shown in Fig. 3h. In the initial growth stage, the sizes of NG facets are relatively small, along with a large number of edge defects, therefore, a remarkable D-band peak appears. When the deposition time increases to 5 min, the NG facets merge together to form a continuous film. Therefore, the contribution in Raman scattering from the edge of the NG facets can be neglected and the corresponding D-band is scarcely observed. Besides, as the growth time increases, the ID/IG value is decreased, on the contrary, the I2D/IG value is increased (Fig. S13). The self-limiting growth process of NG film [29], as the further increase of the growth time, is also observed in our experiment, as shown in Fig. S14. From the AFM, Raman spectrum and transmittance results of NG films synthesized at 5 min and 10 min, it is concluded that once a full coverage of NG has formed (5 min), continued CVD cannot lead to the additional formation of NG film. This may be originated from the significant reduction of active grain edges after fully stitching grain boundaries, resulting in no available sites for new adatom deposition.

To study the electronic properties and doping stability of the NG film, back-gated graphene field-effect transistors (GFETs) are fabricated with the synthesized NG film, and the counterpart of GFETs fabricated using PG film is used for comparison. After the transfer of NG film onto a 300 nm-thick SiO2/highly doped p-type Si substrate, Au/Ti (50/10 nm) are deposited by electron beam evaporation to form source and drain electrodes. Then, photolithography and reactive ion etching (RIE) are used to define the channel area (5 μm × 2 μm) of the field-effect transistor, as shown in Fig. 4a. Fig. 4b shows the IDS varying as a function of the V G. For the PG-based GFET, typical symmetric IDS-VG curve is found, with the charge neutrality between electron and hole conductance locating at nearly zero volt (blue line). However, for the NG film based GFETs, the corresponding charge neutrality shifts to a negative gate voltage (red line), indicating that the utilized graphene is n-type [30], which is coincident to the XPS results in Fig. 2. In addition, an obvious asymmetry in electron and hole conduction is also observed in the NG film based GFET. The doping stability of the NG film is studied by measuring the neutrality points of corresponding GFETs annealed for different time durations, as shown in Fig. 4c. A negligible shift in neutral point is observed with the increase of the annealing time, and the electronic transport properties of the GFETs are almost unchanged even after annealing for 36 h, indicating that the dopants are stably incorporated in the honeycomb lattice plane of graphene. Fig. 4d displays the histogram of the neutrality point obtained from 25 NG film based GFETs, and the average neutrality points (V G) are about −16 V [31,32].

The ability for directly functionalizing NG film into optoelectronic devices represents great significance for its practical applications. In this regard, infrared photodetectors are fabricated with the synthesized NG film on SiO2/Si substrates. Fig. 5a illustrates the schematic diagram of the NG-based photodetector. The current-voltage (I-V) curves of the photodetector with (black) and without (red) the near-infrared light (1550 nm) illumination is shown in Fig. 5b. When the photodetector is illuminated, a significant enhancement of the current is observed. The photoswitching response of the photodetector to a pulsed light is evaluated in Fig. 5c. Our fabricated photodetector with NG film shows prominent response rates and long-term reproducibility [33], with satisfying detectivity (~1.52 × 1010 cm Hz1/2 W−1) and responsivity (79 mA W−1). Pulsed light illumination with a much higher frequency (1 kHz), which is realized by using a chopper, is further utilized to study the photoresponse of the photodetector. As shown in Fig. 5d, the photodetector can track the variations of the pulsed light well. Magnifications of the rising and fall periods are shown in Fig. 5e and Fig. 5f, and the rise time (tr) and fall time (tf) are assessed as 10 and 9 μs, respectively, which is much faster than that of photodetectors fabricated with graphene with lightly doping concentration [34–36].

In summary, we demonstrate a simple and convenient approach to prepare monolayer NG, with N-GQDs as the pre-seeds, via CVD. The presented results show that our proposed approach can introduce dopant atoms in graphene lattices and achieve a uniform substitution. The synthesized NG film is uniformly doped with N, and the doping level of N is as high as 32%. Furthermore, AFM results of NG films at different growth period indicates that N-GQDs serve as the nucleation centers for the growth of NG films. To illustrate the potential applications of the synthesized NG film, photodetectors with high detectivity and responsivity is fabricated. Our study may provide a controllable and convenient approach to achieve doped graphene, which paves the way for the application of graphene in the field of microelectronics.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by projects from National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant (Nos. 11704204, 61604084, and 51802337), the State Key Laboratory of ASIC & System (Grant No. 2020KF007), K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University and the Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo under Grant (No. 2017A610104).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of N-GQDs; synthesis of N-doped NG film; AFM image and corresponding Raman analysis of NG film at different temperatures; AFM images and corresponding Raman analysis of NG film with different ratio of the concentration of hydrogen to methane; Raman spectra of the obtained NG film under different annealing conditions; experimental thermal process schematic for the growth of the NG film; Raman spectra of NG film before and after transferring; chemical structure analysis of N-GQDs; I2D/IG peak ratio distribution histogram of NG film; shift in 2D band and G band of NG film with a function of spin-coating speeds; XPS spectra for the NG film using different spin-coating speeds; optical images of PG and NG film; particle size distribution of NG film; AFM images at different growth time; ID/IG and I2D/IG peak ratios with different growth time; self-limiting growth of NG film; atomic percentages of N graphite N, pyridine N and pyrrole N at different spin coating speeds.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2021.108774.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: [1] A. K. Geim, K. S. Novoselov. The rise of graphene. Nature Materials, 2007, 6(3): 183-191.
[2] A. K. Geim. Graphene: status and prospects. Science, 2009, 324(5934): 1530-1534.
[3] F. Schwierz. Graphene transistors. Nature Nanotechnology, 2010, 5(7): 487-496.
[4] F. Bonaccorso, Z. Sun, T. Hasan, A. C. Ferrari. Graphene photonics and optoelectronics. Nature Photonics, 2010, 4(9): 611-622.

[5] K. S. Novoselov, D. Jiang, F. Schedin, T. J. Booth, V. V. Khotkevich, S. V. Morozov, A. K. Geim. Two-dimensional atomic crystals. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 2005, 102(30): 10451-10453.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electric field effect in atomically thin carbon films

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: [6] K. S. Novoselov, A. K. Geim, S. V. Morozov, D. Jiang, Y. Zhang, S. Dubonos, I. Grigorieva, A. A. Firsov. Electric field effect in atomically thin carbon films. Science, 2004, 306(5696): 666-669.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Experimental observation of the quantum hall effect and berry's phase in graphene

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: [7] Y. Zhang, Y. Tan, H. L. Stormer, P. Kim. Experimental observation of the quantum hall effect and berry's phase in graphene. Nature, 2005, 438(7065): 201-204.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nucleation and growth of single crystal graphene on hexagonal boron nitride

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: [8] S. Tang, G. Ding, X. Xie, J. Chen, C. Wang, X. Ding, F. Huang, W. Lu, M. Jiang. Nucleation and growth of single crystal graphene on hexagonal boron nitride. Carbon, 2012, 50(1): 329-331.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Controllable seeding of single crystal graphene islands from graphene oxide flakes

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Continuous graphene films synthesized at low temperatures by introducing coronene as nucleation seeds
